_note: ORIGINAL content. javascript.info and Eloquent JavaScript informed topic coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Objects
summary: Keep related values together under names you choose.
refresher: A JavaScript object stores values under keys. Use dot notation when the key is written directly in your code. Use brackets when the key comes from a variable. Assigning an object to another variable copies the reference, not a fresh object.

blocks.body: An object is a small bundle of named facts. Instead of remembering that index 0 means name and index 1 means credits, you can write `student.name` and `student.credits`.
Use a dot when you know the property name while writing the code. Use brackets when the property name is stored in a variable. That one choice prevents a lot of quiet bugs.

blocks.caption: Dot notation reads a fixed name, while brackets use the string stored in field.
Use brackets when the property name comes from a variable.

blocks.body: In the wrong version, JavaScript searches for a property literally named `key`. In the fixed version, JavaScript looks inside the variable `key`, finds `"credits"`, and uses that as the property name.

blocks.title: A missing property is quiet at first
blocks.body: Reading a missing property gives you undefined. The error often shows up on the next line, when your code tries to use that missing value as if it were real.

blocks.prompt: Which uses the value in key?
blocks.why: Brackets evaluate the variable, while dot notation searches for a property literally named key.
Both variables refer to one object, so the property change is visible through first.

blocks.title: Objects checklist
items: Use dots for property names written in the code.
Use brackets when the key comes from a variable.
Check optional data before going deeper.
Copy the object first if you need a separate object.
